name: Boehm-Demers-Weiser GC — Conservative Tracing for Unmodified C/C++
summary: Hans Boehm, Alan Demers, and Mark Weiser's BDW collector (1988+) is the canonical conservative tracing GC and the reason GC is even an option for languages that 'don't have one'.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Hans Boehm, Alan Demers, and Mark Weiser's **BDW collector** (1988+) is the canonical conservative tracing GC and the reason GC is even an option for languages that "don't have one."

*Conservative* means the collector treats every machine word as potentially a pointer — it has no static type information, so it cannot tell pointers from integers and must assume any value matching a heap address is a live reference. This trades some precision for the property that **C and C++ programs need no source modification**.

sections.title: Conservative root scanning
sections.content:
BDW intercepts `malloc`/`realloc`/`free` (or replaces them via `LD_PRELOAD`-style hooks) and walks roots from CPU registers, the call stacks of all threads, and global data segments by **scanning bit patterns**.

The precision cost is bounded in practice — Boehm's empirical analyses show conservative misidentification typically retains <5% extra memory on real programs — but unbounded in the worst case (an integer that happens to match a heap address keeps an object alive indefinitely). To mitigate this, BDW supports **typed allocation** (`GC_malloc_typed`) where the program declares per-object pointer layout, restoring per-field precision while keeping the conservative root scan. Generational and incremental modes are available; the collector compiles on virtually every Unix and Windows platform.

sections.title: Comparison — BDW vs. precise tracers vs. MPS
sections.content:
Distinct from precise tracing GCs (Immix, ZGC, Shenandoah, G1, Go, etc.): BDW does **not** require type metadata at every heap allocation, write barriers, or compiler cooperation — exactly the properties that make it deployable in unmodified C/C++ codebases.

Distinct from MPS: BDW is one collector, MPS is a framework of pluggable pool classes. BDW is also distinct from MMTk: MMTk is a framework targeting precise GC for managed languages; BDW is a single conservative collector targeting unmanaged languages.

sections.title: Production deployments
sections.content:
Production: GCJ (Java to native via GCC), historical Mono runtime (replaced by SGen in Mono 2.8, 2010), Mozilla SpiderMonkey's xpconnect bridge, many Scheme implementations (Chicken, MIT/GNU, Bigloo, Gambit), Inferno OS's Limbo runtime, Crystal language runtime, and ~20 years of embedded scripting where adding a precise GC was infeasible.

Status (as of 2026-04): still actively maintained and the conservative-GC reference for languages and runtimes that did not include GC support from the start. Alloy (research GC for Rust, OOPSLA 2025) uses BDWGC as its underlying conservative collector.

sources.label: BDW GC issues and design notes
Boehm & Weiser, "Garbage Collection in an Uncooperative Environment" (SP&E 1988)
